news_summary: Scientists have successfully detected the atmosphere of a rocky, temperate planet for the first time, marking a major breakthrough in the study of potentially habitable worlds.
news_summary_fr: Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont réussi à détecter l'atmosphère d'une planète rocheuse au climat tempéré, ce qui marque une avancée majeure dans l'étude des mondes potentiellement habitables.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado detectar por primera vez la atmósfera de un planeta rocoso de clima templado, lo que supone un gran avance en el estudio de los mundos potencialmente habitables.

articles.title: We’ve found a rocky, temperate planet’s atmosphere for the first time
articles.summary: Astronomers have only previously found atmospheres around exoplanets that are very large or incredibly hot – but now they have found one adorning a world that may well be right for life
articles.content:
Astronomers have only previously found atmospheres around exoplanets that are very large or incredibly hot – but now they have found one adorning a world that may well be right for life

An artist impression of exoplanet LHS 1140 b Melissa Weiss/Center for Astrophysics/Harvard & Smithsonian

For the first time, astronomers have detected an atmosphere on a rocky, temperate world beyond our solar system. It is the strongest evidence yet for an exoplanet that could be able to support alien life.

The planet in question, called LHS 1140b, orbits a small, cool type of star called an M dwarf, about 50 light years from Earth. Researchers have been searching for atmosphere-shrouded planets around M dwarfs for decades because they are the most common type of star in the galaxy, but with no luck until now – some researchers had even postulated that planets around these small stars might not be able to hold onto atmospheres.

But when Collin Cherubim at Harvard University and his colleagues observed LHS 1140b in 2024 at the Las Campanas Observatory in Chile, they found a promising signal: a line in the spectrum of the planet’s light indicating helium atoms in an excited energy state. There are several possible explanations for this, but the shape and strength of the signal led them to conclude that the helium was part of an outflow, leaking away from a planetary atmosphere. “By far the most simple explanation is that this helium that we see is from the planet itself,” says Cherubim.

Advertisement

This would put LHS 1140b in the category of helium worlds, a type of exoplanet that has been proposed but not definitively detected. “It’s different from what we would expect given the rocky planets in our own solar system,” says Natalie Allen at Johns Hopkins University in Maryland. In some ways, a helium world could be more similar to a gas giant than any of the terrestrial worlds in our solar system, with a thick, heavy layer of atmosphere overlaying a rocky or icy surface.

“It’s exciting on its own merits, not as a new Earth,” says Sukrit Ranjan at the University of Arizona. “It might be habitable, but not in a conventional sense.” If it does have a thick atmosphere, pressures at the surface could be similar to those at the bottom of the ocean. If not, it could potentially be an ocean world more similar to icy moons in our solar system than any of the planets.

Right now, we only know that this world has an atmosphere that is leaking helium, but we know very little about the size and composition of the atmosphere itself. “Earth and Venus are very similar in size and both have atmospheres, but Venus’s surface isn’t habitable,” says Allen. “I think to have a real picture of habitability on this planet, we need to understand the bulk atmosphere.” As-yet-unpublished data from the James Webb Space Telescope could help clear that up, she says.

However, the very presence of an atmosphere, along with the fact that LHS 1140b orbits its star in what is known as the habitable zone, where temperatures are mild enough to preserve liquid water on its surface, makes it extremely promising. “This planet has now become the best place to search for life outside of our solar system,” says Cherubim.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Sign up to newsletter

There is a caveat, though. When Cherubim and his colleagues observed LHS 1140b again with the same setup in 2025, they didn’t see the signature helium line. They pored over the 2024 and 2025 data, consulted with other researchers and even had a separate team reanalyse all the data, and the results remained the same: the excited helium was there in 2024, and not in 2025.

That was surprising, but not altogether abnormal, says Cherubim. “When you make a measurement of escaping helium and then you go back and look at it months and years later, it is usually different and sometimes it isn’t there – and we don’t understand it,” he says.

This particular helium signature is poorly understood, says Ranjan, so we can’t be sure why it might have appeared and then disappeared. It is possible that the shape of the outflow changed, or that stellar activity shifted the nature of the helium in the outflow. If the helium is still there but not in an excited state, we would expect the signature to disappear. Cherubim has plans to continue observing LHS 1140b in an attempt to figure it out: “Maybe we’ll never see it again, maybe we’ll see it every time, maybe we’ll see it half the time, but I’m feeling really good about it,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the first detection of an atmosphere on a rocky, temperate exoplanet, LHS 1140b, which is a significant breakthrough in the search for potentially habitable worlds beyond our solar system. This discovery has broad scientific and public interest implications, inspiring hope for finding life elsewhere and advancing understanding of planetary atmospheres. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, its significance, and future research plans, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers detect atmosphere on a rocky, temperate exoplanet for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have found atmospheres before, but only on very large or extremely hot planets outside our solar system. Until now, no one had confirmed an atmosphere on a smaller, rocky planet with mild temperatures that could support life.

**What happened** – In 2024, astronomers led by Collin Cherubim at Harvard observed a planet called LHS 1140b, about 50 light years from Earth, orbiting a small, cool star. They detected helium gas escaping from this planet’s atmosphere. This was the first time an atmosphere was found on a rocky, temperate exoplanet.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because LHS 1140b orbits in the “habitable zone,” where temperatures might allow liquid water to exist. The planet’s atmosphere is unusual, possibly thick and rich in helium, unlike anything in our solar system. This makes it a unique place to study and a promising candidate in the search for life beyond Earth.

**What's next step** – Astronomers will continue to observe LHS 1140b to understand its atmosphere better. Upcoming data from the James Webb Space Telescope may reveal more about the planet’s atmosphere and whether it could truly support life. Researchers also want to understand why the helium signature was missing in observations made the following year.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have found signs of an atmosphere on a rocky planet with mild temperatures outside our solar system, opening new possibilities to study planets that might support life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes détectent pour la première fois une atmosphère sur une exoplanète rocheuse et tempérée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques avaient déjà trouvé des atmosphères autour de grosses planètes très chaudes en dehors de notre système solaire, mais jamais sur une planète plus petite, rocheuse et avec des températures modérées qui pourraient permettre la vie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, une équipe menée par Collin Cherubim à Harvard a observé une planète appelée LHS 1140b, située à environ 50 années-lumière de la Terre, en orbite autour d’une petite étoile froide. Ils ont détecté de l’hélium s’échappant de l’atmosphère de cette planète. C’est la première fois qu’une atmosphère est trouvée sur une exoplanète rocheuse et tempérée.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car LHS 1140b se trouve dans la « zone habitable », où la température pourrait permettre la présence d’eau liquide. Son atmosphère est inhabituelle, peut-être épaisse et riche en hélium, différente de celles des planètes de notre système solaire. Cela en fait un lieu unique à étudier et un candidat prometteur dans la recherche de vie ailleurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les astronomes vont continuer à observer LHS 1140b pour mieux comprendre son atmosphère. Les prochaines données du télescope spatial James Webb pourraient révéler davantage sur cette atmosphère et son potentiel pour la vie. Les chercheurs cherchent aussi à comprendre pourquoi la trace d’hélium a disparu lors des observations de l’année suivante.

**Une phrase clé** – Pour la première fois, les scientifiques ont trouvé des signes d’atmosphère sur une planète rocheuse aux températures modérées en dehors de notre système solaire, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour étudier des planètes susceptibles d’abriter la vie.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos detectan por primera vez atmósfera en un exoplaneta rocoso y templado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos antes habían encontrado atmósferas solo en planetas muy grandes o muy calientes fuera de nuestro sistema solar, pero nunca en un planeta pequeño, rocoso y con temperaturas suaves que podrían permitir la vida.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, un equipo liderado por Collin Cherubim en Harvard observó un planeta llamado LHS 1140b, a unos 50 años luz de la Tierra, que orbita una estrella pequeña y fría. Detectaron helio escapando de la atmósfera de este planeta. Es la primera vez que se encuentra una atmósfera en un exoplaneta rocoso y templado.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque LHS 1140b está en la «zona habitable», donde las temperaturas podrían mantener agua líquida. Su atmósfera es inusual, posiblemente gruesa y rica en helio, diferente a las de los planetas de nuestro sistema solar. Esto lo convierte en un lugar único para estudiar y un candidato prometedor en la búsqueda de vida fuera de la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los astrónomos seguirán observando LHS 1140b para entender mejor su atmósfera. Los datos que pronto enviará el telescopio espacial James Webb podrían revelar más sobre la atmósfera del planeta y su capacidad para albergar vida. También quieren descubrir por qué la señal de helio desapareció en observaciones posteriores.

**Una frase clave** – Por primera vez, los científicos encontraron señales de una atmósfera en un planeta rocoso con temperaturas suaves fuera de nuestro sistema solar, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para estudiar planetas que podrían tener vida.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a distant rocky exoplanet with a softly glowing, translucent atmosphere gently leaking wisps of golden helium gas into space, orbiting a small, cool red dwarf star that casts a calm, amber light over the planet’s rugged surface and faint ocean-like reflections.
